content.text: Streaming responses are a delivery mechanism in which an AI agent sends its output to the client incrementally — as tokens are generated — rather than accumulating the full reply and sending it in one batch once inference is complete. The model produces tokens one at a time through an autoregressive loop; streaming exposes that loop's output directly in real time over a persistent HTTP connection.

In practice, the server holds the HTTP response open and writes small data chunks — typically one or a few tokens — each time the model emits them. The client reads these chunks as they arrive and renders them progressively. The two most common transport mechanisms are
— a one-directional text protocol built on HTTP/1.1 chunked transfer encoding — and
, which provide full-duplex communication. Most LLM APIs, including the Anthropic and OpenAI APIs, default to SSE.
Each chunk arrives as a newline-delimited message such as
. The client parses these events, extracts the text delta, and appends it to whatever is being rendered. A special

The core UX problem streaming solves is latency perception. A high-quality LLM response for a complex query might take 15–30 seconds to generate fully. Without streaming, the user sees nothing for that entire duration, then the full answer appears. With streaming, the first words appear within one second (sometimes under 500 ms) and continue flowing until completion. The total generation time is unchanged — but the experience is qualitatively different.

In agentic AI systems this is compounded by chain-of-thought and multi-step reasoning, which can take far longer than simple completions. Streaming allows the agent to signal progress: the user can watch reasoning unfold, see intermediate planning steps, and catch early signs of misunderstanding — giving them the opportunity to interrupt before the agent commits to a costly tool call or generates a large body of unusable text.
Beyond user experience, streaming enables an entirely different class of architectures: agents that

output. A downstream system can begin processing, translating, or re-rendering the response before the upstream generator has finished, reducing end-to-end wall-clock latency across complex multi-agent pipelines.

Time-to-first-token (TTFT) is often under one second. The user sees the response begin immediately, reducing abandonment and increasing perceived responsiveness even when total generation time is unchanged.

The client or orchestrator can close the connection as soon as it has seen enough output — for instance, once a tool call or decision is visible — without waiting for the full response. This saves tokens and compute.
Downstream stages (text-to-speech, translation, embedding, another LLM) can start consuming partial output as it arrives, overlapping work and cutting total pipeline latency.
Neither the server nor the client needs to buffer the entire response in memory before acting on it. This matters for very long outputs such as code generation or document drafting.
Watching the model's reasoning stream out — especially in chain-of-thought mode — builds user trust by making the agent's thought process visible rather than opaque.
Active streaming connections keep network proxies and load balancers alive through keep-alive signals, avoiding idle-connection timeouts that would otherwise kill long-running completions.
Both the server (async generator, SSE encoding) and the client (event listener, incremental DOM updates) require non-trivial code compared to a simple request/response.

If the connection drops mid-stream, the client has an incomplete response. Retry logic must track the last-received position or restart cleanly — there is no atomic 'success or failure' like a normal HTTP 200.
Standard HTTP caches cannot cache streaming responses. Semantic caching (caching complete outputs for later replay) must be built manually on top of the stream.
If the model generates tokens faster than the client can consume them, buffers can grow. The server must implement flow control rather than relying on the model to self-throttle.

Streaming requires a long-lived connection, which is harder to proxy, load-balance, and route than stateless short requests. CDN edge nodes that terminate HTTP/1.1 may buffer responses unless explicitly configured otherwise.
If a stream fails at token 300 of a 500-token response, you cannot resume from token 300 — autoregressive generation must restart from the beginning.

At the model level, a transformer LLM generates text autoregressively: each forward pass through the network produces a probability distribution over the vocabulary, from which one token is sampled. This token is appended to the context, and the process repeats. Because tokens are produced sequentially, the model naturally produces output one token at a time — streaming exposes this natural cadence directly to the caller.
At the transport level, the server does not set a

header (which would require knowing the full size upfront). Instead it uses HTTP chunked transfer encoding: each chunk is a hex-length prefix followed by the chunk bytes, then CRLF. The browser or HTTP client reassembles chunks transparently.
SSE wraps chunked transfer with a simple text protocol. The server sets
and writes newline-delimited events. Each event may have
fields, followed by a blank line. The browser's

The inter-token latency follows an approximately constant rate once the model reaches cruising speed. For typical hosted LLM APIs, TTFT is 150–600 ms and inter-token latency is roughly 15–30 ms (40–70 tokens/second). A 500-token response takes approximately 8–15 seconds total — long enough to make streaming essential for any interactive product.
The diagram below shows how tokens flow from the LLM model through the agent server to the client, using SSE over HTTP chunked transfer encoding.

attrs.alt: Streaming response architecture: Client sends POST to Agent Server which uses an async generator and SSE encoder to forward token stream from the LLM Model back to the Client progressively.

content.text: Key flow: (1) The client issues a single HTTP POST with

. (2) The agent server opens an upstream streaming request to the LLM model and immediately begins forwarding SSE chunks downstream. (3) The LLM model runs its autoregressive decoding loop, emitting one token per forward pass. (4) Each token travels: model → agent (token stream) → client (SSE chunk). (5) A terminal event signals stream completion.
Any interface where a human waits for a reply benefits from visible progressive output. The chat-typing metaphor maps naturally to the streaming model.
Code generation, document drafting, and multi-paragraph explanations — where total generation time may be 20 seconds or more — make streaming nearly mandatory for usability.
When one agent's output feeds into another's input, streaming allows the second agent to start immediately rather than waiting for full upstream completion.
When using extended thinking or chain-of-thought modes, streaming lets operators see reasoning as it develops and optionally surface it to users.
TTS synthesis can begin on the first sentence of streamed text while the rest continues generating, enabling sub-second audio start latency.
A safety filter can inspect the token stream and interrupt generation the instant a policy violation is detected, saving compute and eliminating the need to discard a fully-generated unsafe reply.

appropriate for batch workloads (where latency is irrelevant), very short responses (where TTFT advantage is negligible), or environments where structured JSON output must be fully validated before processing.
Implementation has three layers: calling the LLM API with streaming enabled, building a server-side streaming endpoint, and consuming the stream on the client.
1. Call the LLM API in streaming mode

# pip install anthropic
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

# client.messages.stream() returns a context manager over MessageStreamEvent objects
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain photosynthesis in detail."}],
) as stream:
    for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
        # text_chunk is a plain string — the incremental text delta
        print(text_chunk, end="", flush=True)

# After the context exits, the fully assembled Message is available
final_message = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"\nStop reason: {final_message.stop_reason}")
print(f"Input tokens: {final_message.usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {final_message.usage.output_tokens}")

# pip install fastapi uvicorn anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import anthropic
import json

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.Anthropic()


async def token_generator(user_message: str, request: Request):
    """Async generator that yields SSE-formatted chunks from the LLM."""
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    ) as stream:
        for text_chunk in stream.text_stream:
            # Propagate client disconnection upstream to avoid wasting tokens
            if await request.is_disconnected():
                break
            payload = json.dumps({"type": "delta", "text": text_chunk})
            yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"

    yield 'data: {"type": "done"}\n\n'


@app.post("/stream")
async def stream_completion(body: dict, request: Request):
    return StreamingResponse(
        token_generator(body.get("message", ""), request),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disables nginx buffering
        },
    )
# Run with: uvicorn main:app --reload
3. Consume the stream in the browser
The raw SSE wire format that a streaming endpoint produces over an open HTTP connection looks like this:

Notice that chunk boundaries do not align with word boundaries — the model emits tokens, not words or sentences. A client buffer must accumulate text correctly rather than treating each SSE event as a complete word or sentence.
Low-level raw event iteration is also possible when you need access to non-text events such as tool-use blocks:
Consider a coding assistant that takes a natural-language description and streams back a Python implementation. The pipeline has three stages that overlap in time thanks to streaming:
receives the user's request and immediately opens a streaming request to the LLM. It begins forwarding SSE chunks to the browser before any code has been fully written.

renders arriving tokens into a syntax-highlighted code block. The user can read and mentally evaluate the approach while the function body is still being generated. If the approach is wrong, they click a stop button to terminate the stream.

on the server parses each arriving chunk looking for syntactically complete function definitions. When a full function is detected mid-stream, it submits it to a sandboxed test runner in parallel — so test results may arrive only seconds after the stream concludes.

# Production streaming with latency instrumentation
import anthropic, time, asyncio, json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import AsyncGenerator

aclient = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()


@dataclass
class StreamMetrics:
    start: float = field(default_factory=time.monotonic)
    first_token_at: float = 0.0
    last_token_at: float = 0.0
    token_count: int = 0

    @property
    def ttft_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.first_token_at - self.start) * 1000

    @property
    def tps(self) -> float:
        dur = self.last_token_at - self.first_token_at
        return self.token_count / dur if dur > 0 else 0.0


async def instrumented_stream(messages: list) -> AsyncGenerator[str, None]:
    metrics = StreamMetrics()
    async with aclient.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=2048, messages=messages
    ) as stream:
        async for chunk in stream.text_stream:
            now = time.monotonic()
            if metrics.token_count == 0:
                metrics.first_token_at = now
            metrics.last_token_at = now
            metrics.token_count += 1
            yield chunk
    # Emit to observability system
    print(
        f"ttft={metrics.ttft_ms:.0f}ms "
        f"tps={metrics.tps:.1f} "
        f"tokens={metrics.token_count}"
    )

This architecture reduces the perceived time from user request to first readable output by approximately 90% compared to a non-streaming design for a 1000-token response, without changing the underlying model or prompt.
Buffering the entire response in middleware.
Proxy layers (nginx, API gateways) often buffer responses to calculate Content-Length. This completely negates streaming. Always set
for nginx and confirm your gateway passes chunks through immediately.
Some WSGI and ASGI frameworks buffer output for efficiency. Verify that each
in your generator is immediately flushed to the TCP stack. FastAPI with uvicorn flushes automatically; Flask with WSGI may not.
Splitting on word or newline boundaries client-side.
SSE chunks contain raw token strings, which may split words. Append each chunk to a string accumulator and let the UI re-render the whole string. Never split on spaces.
If the client does not handle a
sentinel, the connection stays open until a timeout. Always close or clean up on terminal events.
Using synchronous code in an async generator.
Calling a blocking IO function inside an
generator blocks the entire event loop, stalling all other concurrent streams. Use
When an LLM takes a long time before emitting the first token (e.g., during extended thinking), the connection may time out. Send periodic comment events (
If a browser tab closes mid-stream, detect the disconnect (
in FastAPI) and cancel the upstream API call to avoid paying for tokens no user will ever read.
on errors. Never silently close the connection.
If the LLM API call fails mid-stream, catch the exception in the generator and yield an error event before returning. The client should display the partial response plus an error notice.

Synchronous WSGI frameworks cannot handle long-lived streaming connections efficiently. Use FastAPI, Starlette, or Quart with an ASGI server (uvicorn, hypercorn) to handle many concurrent streams on a small number of threads.
Set appropriate timeouts at every layer.
Configure read timeouts on the LLM API client, write timeouts on the HTTP server, and connection timeouts on the client. A stuck stream should not silently consume a connection indefinitely.
Log stream start and end, not individual chunks.
Logging every token is prohibitively expensive. Log the start of a stream (with request metadata), any errors, and the completion event (with total tokens and wall-clock duration).
on streaming responses to prevent caching and proxy buffering.

Each streaming connection holds an open file descriptor, an async task slot, and associated state for the duration of the response. On a busy agent server, you may have thousands of concurrent streams, each lasting 5–30 seconds.

ASGI with async generators handles thousands of concurrent streams on a handful of OS threads because each stream spends most of its time awaiting IO, not consuming CPU. A thread-per-connection model does not scale to this pattern.
The LLM API client should reuse HTTP/2 connections with multiplexing. Opening a new TCP connection per request adds 50–150 ms and limits throughput under load.
If your server is horizontally scaled, the load balancer must route all requests for a given streaming session to the same backend. A stream that bounces between backends will break.
Sending one SSE event per token is fine for most use cases. For throughput-dominated batch pipelines, batch tokens into slightly larger chunks to reduce per-event overhead.

Instrument streams with TTFT, tokens-per-second throughput, and total duration. High TTFT usually indicates upstream API latency or queue congestion; low throughput indicates network or CPU constraints.
for all streaming endpoints. The default 60-second idle timeout silently truncates long responses.
, a context manager that yields typed event objects and exposes
for text-only iteration. Handles SSE parsing, reconnection, and final message assembly.
— wraps any Python async generator as a streaming HTTP response with chunked transfer encoding. Compatible with ASGI middleware.
supports async iteration over response bytes without blocking the event loop.
— both frameworks expose streaming callbacks and async generators at the chain and graph level, propagating token events through multi-step pipelines automatically.
— the native browser SSE client. Auto-reconnects using
, but only supports GET requests. For POST-based streaming, use
— a TypeScript library for building streaming AI UIs in React/Next.js. Provides
hooks that handle SSE parsing, buffering, and re-rendering automatically.
— use span attributes for TTFT and tokens-per-second to trace streaming performance across distributed multi-agent systems.
Batch jobs, short responses, structured output
Environments where SSE is blocked by proxy

Validate the auth token on the initial HTTP request, not per-chunk. If a token is revoked mid-stream, close the stream at the next yield point rather than allowing it to continue until the connection drops naturally.
JSON-encode LLM output in SSE data fields.
A malicious prompt could attempt to inject SSE control characters (
in a data field) to forge fake events. Always
token text before placing it in the
field. Never embed raw LLM output as an unencoded SSE value.

A client that reads extremely slowly can cause the server's generator send buffer to fill. Set a maximum stream duration and a maximum send buffer size, and close the connection if either is exceeded.
Never act on a truncated stream.
Never persist or act on the content of a stream that was not cleanly terminated with a
event. A truncated response may be syntactically invalid (cut-off JSON, incomplete code) and semantically misleading.
Rate limit by connection, not by byte.

Apply rate limits at the connection-open level. One streaming connection can legitimately consume many tokens; rate-limit by requests-per-minute or concurrent-connections-per-user rather than bytes-per-second.
Even with streaming, enforce an absolute wall-clock timeout on each upstream LLM call so a misbehaving call cannot hold a connection open indefinitely and exhaust your concurrency budget.

Think of a streaming LLM response like a radio broadcast rather than a downloaded podcast. In a podcast (batch mode), you wait for the entire recording to be available before you can press play. In a radio broadcast (streaming mode), audio plays the instant you tune in and the content continues to arrive in real time.
The key insight is that the LLM was
generating output one token at a time. Streaming does not change how the model works; it changes

. In batch mode the buffer sits at the server, which holds the whole response before sending. In streaming mode the buffer moves to the client, which accumulates just enough to render the next incremental update.
Streaming trades server-side buffer completeness for client-side render latency. The total work is identical — only the timing of visibility shifts.
A second model useful for agentic systems: the token stream is a

. An agent supervisor reading this channel can make decisions in real time — fire a tool call the moment its parameters are complete, trigger an alarm if the model begins hallucinating, or update a progress indicator. In batch mode that supervisor is blind until the broadcast is entirely over.
Streaming does not change how an LLM generates output — it changes where the buffer boundary sits, moving it from the server to the client so tokens appear as they are produced.
Server-Sent Events over HTTP chunked transfer encoding is the standard mechanism: low complexity, broad browser support, and automatic reconnection via the

The primary benefit is dramatically lower perceived latency (time-to-first-token), which makes long-running LLM calls feel interactive and allows users to terminate early if the response goes in the wrong direction.
In multi-agent pipelines, streaming enables pipeline parallelism: a downstream agent can start consuming and acting on partial output before the upstream generator finishes.
Correct implementation requires async generators (ASGI), disabled buffering at every proxy layer, properly encoded SSE format, and a sentinel terminal event to signal clean completion.
Always JSON-encode LLM output before embedding it in SSE data fields to prevent injection of control characters, and never treat a truncated stream as a valid complete response.

Streaming trades operational simplicity (no caching, stateful connections, more complex error handling) for a qualitatively better user experience — in interactive agent applications the trade-off is almost always worth making.
Instrument TTFT and tokens-per-second as separate SLO metrics; they expose different parts of the system and have different failure modes.
